You are VERIFYING that an applied edit sweep landed correctly on heat BZ's citizen-model artifacts. Ground truth is the files ON DISK — READ them; do not trust any summary. Files: Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md (the mechanics memo, just rewritten), Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc (roadmap, 2 prose edits), Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md (a breadcrumb added near its Status). The paddock is gallops-stored and is being verified separately by the main agent — do NOT flag paddock-only concerns. The sweep was meant to apply these findings (id: expected-change [target]):
- 1: SA-naming migration section — identity-only SA names, enumerate RBSDK/RBSRK/RBSDD/RBSDR/RBSRA surfaces, delete+recreate cutover via rbk-08 revoke layer+Class-C [memo]
- 2: member-first audit axis (enumerate actual members on repo/bucket/SA policies, flag non-ledger/non-system); correct audit-domain line; soften repoAdmin 'covers' claim [memo]
- 3: divest/remove = withdraw ledger intent FIRST then revoke IAM then delete SA; 'no IAM revoke without prior ledger withdrawal' invariant [memo]
- 4: citizen vs RBSHR — RBSHR artifact-'citizen' prose reworded to 'holding'; memo note recording the supersede [RBSHR+memo]
- 5: rename 'declared roster' -> 'declared ledger' throughout, disambiguated from the actual-reading roster verb; audit = diff(actual-state roster, declared ledger) [memo]
- 6: capability-set definition EXPANSION gated behind human adjudication (vs per-identity failed-grant auto-heal); 'who edits definitions' is first-class authority [memo]
- 7: 10-keys-per-SA quota; rekey order create-new->deliver->verify->delete-old; reclaim stale keys [memo]
- 8: concurrency — auto-converge re-reads ledger under etag before each grant; teardown writes ledger first [memo]
- 9: half-failed REVOKE handled (idempotent retry / revoking-marker, not adversarial surplus); fix 'no verb auto-revokes' -> 'the audit never auto-revokes' [memo]
- 10: orphan sweep is integrity-advisory; authoritative signal = USER_MANAGED key on an SA absent from the ledger [memo]
- 11: ledger home is NOT the build bucket (director holds objectCreator); write-ACL governor/payor only; cinch 'ledger-write never a capability-set member' [memo]
- 12: Writers rule topology-conditional (writer == grant-authority holder under active topology) [memo]
- 13: 'mantle is not special' qualified to once-governor-is-idempotent [memo]
- 14: migration described as one-time name-based bootstrap, distinct from retired 'derive from IAM' [memo migration section]
- 15: migration stamps orphan marker onto grandfathered legacy SAs [memo]
- 16: ephemeral-governor caveat — revoke strips binding not on-disk key; couple key-destruction [memo]
- 20: federate vs Workload 'federates' seam pointer [memo]
- 22: deferral-honoring breadcrumb added to memo-20260527 [memo-20260527]

Report only genuine problems (missing/partial/regression/inconsistency/stray-rename) on the ON-DISK files. It is fine to report zero issues. Use kind 'ok-note' sparingly only to record a checked-and-clean high-risk item.

YOUR CHECK: CROSS-DOC CONSISTENCY. READ Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc, Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md. Check: (1) the memo's 'supersedes RBSHR prose citizen' note matches the actual RBSHR rewording; (2) the Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md breadcrumb exists, is near Status, names heat BZ and the citizen memo, and honors the 'record when frozen' deferral (must NOT be the full supersede note); (3) the memo's three claimed divergences from Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md are still faithful to Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md's actual text; (4) terminology (citizen/federate/holding/capability-set/declared ledger) is consistent across these docs.

- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

content.content:
1	# Citizen Tier — Identity / Capability Mechanics
2	
3	Date: 2026-06-05
4	
5	Status: Design captured during the 260605 grooming conversation for heat ₣BZ
6	(`rbk-14-citizen-model`). The *shape* lives in the ₣BZ paddock; this memo holds the
7	*mechanism* the paddock points to. Nothing here is built yet. Amended 260605 after two review
8	passes (a single-reviewer pass, then an ultracode multi-agent pass — see
9	memo-20260605-citizen-model-ultracode-process.md).
10	
11	## Scope and vocabulary
12	
13	This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current, keyfile, no-org operator-credential
14	tier — restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's, so
15	that federation plugs in later by swapping identity-kind and token mint and nothing else.
16	
17	- **Citizen** — a keyfile-tier operator identity: a depot-issued service account holding one
18	  RBRA key. Rank-neutral (governor, director, retriever are all citizens); the home of the
19	  identity, not the role. Possession of the key is the identity. (Mints over RBSHR's prose use of
20	  "citizen" for a depot *artifact* — those are reworded to "holding"; minting rule: semantic
21	  uniqueness across all persistent names.)
22	- **Federate** (future, not this heat) — a federation-tier operator identity: an external-IdP
23	  `principal://` subject the depot recognizes by treaty. A federate holds no key. (This is the
24	  *Workforce*-federated operator subject; not the envoy's *Workload* "federates-to-AWS" egress,
25	  which RBSHR Envoy and memo-20260527 Workforce-vs-Workload already separate.)
26	- **Payor** — the founding human (OAuth refresh token), outside both tiers; neither citizen nor
27	  federate (memo-20260527).
28	- **Capability-set** — a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (the role's binding list,
29	  lifted out of today's invest bodies). Tier-blind.
30	- **Declared ledger** — depot-resident data mapping identity → {capability-sets held}: the stored
31	  *intent*. Tier-blind. Distinct from the existing `roster` cult-verb (RBSDR), which reads the
32	  *actual* discovered IAM state — the audit is precisely the diff between the actual-state roster
33	  and the declared ledger. (Short form below: "the ledger.")
34	- **Holdings** — the depot's artifacts (arks/hoards/bullions). Property, not identities. The
35	  depot's civic ontology — citizens/federates (people) + holdings (property) — covers *operator*
36	  identities; cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it, reusing the
37	  capability-set abstraction without the citizen lifecycle (whether they deserve their own
38	  ontological category is deferred).
39	
40	The convergence: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke, the declared ledger,
41	the audit) is tier-blind and shared. The **identity** differs by tier (citizen vs federate) —
42	that difference is the one real seam. "Structural isomorphism" means the capability layer is
43	identical across tiers *modulo the opaque principal handle the identity layer supplies*
44	(`serviceAccount:…` vs `principal://…`); only identity-kind, that handle, and the token mint
45	(signed-JWT vs STS) differ.
46	
47	## Verb dissolution
48	
49	Today's role-keyed verbs decompose into a generic register parameterized by
50	(actor, capability-set, identity):
51	
52	| Today | Decomposes to | Notes |
53	|---|---|---|
54	| invest (director/retriever) | add-citizen + grant(set) | governor is actor |
55	| mantle (governor) | add-citizen + grant(governor-set) | payor is actor; not special *once the governor is idempotent* (see below) |
56	| divest | revoke(all held) + remove-citizen | ledger-withdraw-first (see Identity verbs) |
57	| roster (verb) | read the declared ledger | the actual-reading verb; the audit diffs it against IAM |
58	| — | grant(set) / revoke(set) | the capability axis, tier-blind |
59	| — | rekey | keyfile-only identity-backing maintenance |
60	
61	Actor and capability-set are parameters, not verb variants. Federation reuses the capability
62	half (grant/revoke/ledger-read) verbatim and swaps the identity half (add-federate, no rekey).
63	
64	**mantle is not special only once the governor is idempotent** (stable name, add-once + rekey). If
65	the governor stays datestamped delete+recreate (today's RBSGM), mantle decomposes to
66	*divest + add-citizen + grant* and remains a replace verb — see Governor teardown leak.
67	
68	## Identity verbs branch; capability verbs do not
69	
70	- **Capability verbs (grant/revoke)** never branch on tier — both are an IAM binding on a
71	  pre-existing identity.
72	- **Identity verbs** are tier-specific (this is the seam):
73	  - *citizen* (this heat):
74	    - **add** = mint SA + key + deliver RBRA.
75	    - **rekey** = rotate the key, bindings untouched. Availability-safe order: create-new →
76	      deliver RBRA → verify → delete-old (never delete-before-create — a failed create would leave
77	      a live citizen keyless). GCP caps an SA at **10 keys** (hard, non-adjustable), so a rekey
78	      that fails to reclaim the old key accumulates toward the ceiling; reclaim stale keys, and let
79	      the orphan/audit sweep flag `>1` USER_MANAGED key on a citizen as a stranded key to reclaim.
80	    - **remove** = **withdraw ledger intent (delete the citizen's ledger rows) FIRST**, then revoke
81	      IAM, then delete the SA. Invariant: *no IAM revoke without a prior ledger withdrawal* — the
82	      teardown dual of grant-writes-intent-first. (Ledger-first means a crashed teardown lands as a
83	      transient *surplus* — reported, safe — not an auto-converging *deficit* that would resurrect
84	      the just-removed citizen, acute because RBSDD anticipates immediate same-folio re-invest.)
85	  - *federate* (future): add ≈ first grant (identity owned by the IdP, not minted here); no rekey
86	    (the IdP owns rotation); remove = de-register + revoke-all (the principal is not ours to
87	    delete).
88	  - *payor*: install / refresh a human OAuth refresh token.
89	- Token mint also branches (citizen: signed-JWT; federate: STS) and sits below everything.
90	
91	## The declared ledger
92	
93	Why intent must be stored (the retired "derive from IAM" cinch was self-defeating once the role
94	prefix leaves the identity name):
95	
96	1. Without a name prefix, deriving the ledger from IAM is lossy reverse-mapping — a director set
97	   is several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket; partial and ambiguous
98	   matches become representable.
99	2. You cannot audit IAM against itself — "intent = whatever IAM says" makes intended-vs-actual a
100	   tautology that always passes, discarding the drift signal.
101	
102	Federation forces the same ledger anyway (a federated principal carries no role marker;
103	memo-20260527: "the depot remains the home of the roster"), so the ledger is paid once for both
104	tiers and makes the routine ledger read identical and O(1) across tiers.
105	
106	- **Code vs data.** Capability-set *definitions* are code; the ledger is *data* (memberships).
107	  The audit compares: for each (identity, declared-set), are the code-defined bindings present in
108	  IAM?
109	- **Physical home (open) — a security boundary, not just storage.** The home must place
110	  ledger-write authority at least on par with grant authority (see the audit invariant below).
111	  This **disqualifies the obvious choice**: a GCS object in the depot's *build* bucket fails the
112	  invariant, because the director already holds `roles/storage.objectCreator` there (RBSDK) — a
113	  director could overwrite the ledger and self-escalate via auto-converge. The ledger home's
114	  write-ACL must be reachable by **no capability-set** — held only by governor/payor. Viable
115	  options: a separate access-restricted bucket/object, or a GAR artifact, each with a
116	  governor/payor-only write ACL. Decide in-heat. **Cinch: ledger-write authority is never a
117	  capability-set member.**
118	- **Writers (topology-conditional).** The ledger writer for an entry is the holder of grant
119	  authority for that entry's capability-sets under the active topology.
120	  - Federation/multi-admin: the governor writes director/retriever entries; the payor writes the
121	    governor entry.
122	  - Keyfile/solo: the payor writes director/retriever entries directly; a demoted or absent
123	    governor must NOT write entries it lacks grant authority for.
124	
125	## The audit
126	
127	Drift taxonomy (for one identity; D = union of declared sets' bindings, A = actual RBK-relevant
128	bindings):
129	
130	| State | Meaning | Likely cause | Heal |
131	|---|---|---|---|
132	| D = A | clean | — | — |
133	| deficit-whole | a declared set wholly absent in A | grant never ran / errant revoke | auto-converge |
134	| deficit-fragment | a declared set partly present | half-failed multi-scope grant | auto-converge |
135	| surplus-complete | a whole undeclared named set in A | out-of-band grant | report |
136	| surplus-fragment | bindings belonging to no whole set | tampering / shrunk definition / half-failed revoke | report (but see below) |
137	
138	**Half-failed revoke is ours, not adversarial.** A multi-scope revoke (a director set spans
139	project + Mason + repo + bucket) that fails partway — exactly the Class-C 403-flap memo-20260604
140	documents — leaves a fragment of a no-longer-declared set: structurally a surplus, but *ours*. The
141	divest/remove verb retries its own revoke idempotently (Class-C tolerance) so most flaps resolve
142	(~81s) without stranding a fragment; for a fully abandoned mid-divest, a recorded "revoking" intent
143	marker lets the audit recognize an interrupted authorized revoke as ours and finish it
144	idempotently, distinct from out-of-band surplus.
145	
146	**Asymmetric healing = the Pale recognition test:**
147	
148	- **Deficit is ours** (our own grant failed to complete) → auto-converge up to D (re-run the
149	  idempotent grant), logged, never silent.
150	- **Surplus crossed the Pale** (IAM touched outside our verbs) → characterize, report, let a human
151	  adjudicate (legitimize → declare it, or reject → revoke it). Never auto-backfill intent
152	  (re-enters the tautology). **The *audit* never auto-revokes** — surplus resolution is
153	  human-driven. (Divest/remove *do* revoke; that is an explicit operator teardown, not an audit
154	  heal.)
155	
156	**Invariant the asymmetry rests on.** Auto-converging deficits treats the declared ledger as
157	authoritative intent, so writing the ledger *upward* is itself a grant. The auto-heal is safe only
158	if ledger-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy); otherwise it
159	is a softer escalation path than setIamPolicy. The ledger's physical home (above) must satisfy that
160	ACL constraint — a security boundary, not a storage convenience.
161	
162	**Two audit axes.** The identity-first axis (above) walks `ledger identities × set-defined
163	bindings`. It **cannot see** escalation onto a *fresh, non-ledger* principal — e.g. a leaked
164	director, via repo-scope `repoAdmin`/`setIamPolicy`, grants a role to a brand-new SA the ledger
165	never names. So the audit also runs a **member-first axis**: on every resource where an RBK
166	principal can reach `setIamPolicy` (the AR repo, the build bucket, individual SAs), enumerate the
167	*actual* members and flag any RBK-relevant binding whose member is neither a ledger identity nor an
168	allow-listed system identity (Mason, payor, governor, future Envoy — allow-listed to avoid false
169	positives). The repoAdmin residual (Authority section) is covered **only once this axis exists**.
170	
171	Consequences:
172	
173	- Because grant writes intent *first*, our tooling cannot produce a surplus *absent a definition
174	  change* — so a surplus is normally a clean "someone bypassed the verbs" signal. The one
175	  tooling-side surplus source is a capability-set definition *shrink* (next bullet), which strands a
176	  binding our own earlier grant placed — which is why surplus is reported, not auto-revoked,
177	  regardless of origin.
178	- **A definition change is not all routine.** A *shrink* decomposes to surplus-on-removals (report
179	  for human-confirmed revoke). An *expansion* (adding a binding to a set's definition) turns every
180	  holder into a deficit the audit would auto-grant — silently relocating the privilege-expansion
181	  trust boundary to "who can merge a one-line code change." So distinguish a per-identity deficit
182	  against an *unchanged* definition (a genuine failed grant — safe to auto-heal) from a deficit
183	  appearing across *all* holders of a set at once (the signature of an expansion — gate behind the
184	  same human adjudication that surplus-legitimization requires). **"Who may edit capability-set
185	  definitions" is a first-class authority, equal to grant / ledger-write.**
186	- **Concurrency.** Two writers hit IAM: the grant verb and the audit's deficit auto-converge.
187	  Auto-converge re-reads the ledger under etag immediately before each grant (not from a
188	  start-of-audit snapshot), so a concurrent revoke wins. Both teardown sides (revoke, definition
189	  shrink) write the ledger first so a crash lands as report-only surplus, never an auto-converging
190	  deficit that resurrects what was just removed.
191	- **Audit domain.** Identity-first: ledger identities × set-defined bindings. Member-first: actual
192	  members of setIamPolicy-reachable resource policies. A non-ledger, non-system member is *flagged*,
193	  not ignored.
194	
195	## Orphan hazard and the identity marker (citizen tier only)
196	
197	With no name prefix and no SA labels, nothing intrinsic marks an SA as RBK-managed — so a failed
198	add-citizen can strand a live key for an untracked SA (a credential leak you cannot find).
199	
200	The orphan sweep is **integrity-advisory, not authoritative.** The description sentinel lives in
201	`serviceAccounts.update` — the same `serviceAccountAdmin` authority that mints citizens — so it can
202	be stripped, or absent after a crash. Marker *absence must not be trusted.* The **authoritative**
203	orphan signal is the ledger cross-check the capability audit already performs: a USER_MANAGED key on
204	an SA absent from the declared ledger is an orphan, marker or not. Marker options, used only as
205	corroborating hints:
206	
207	- **SA tags** (tag bindings): key-value, listable, condition-usable, with a separable (stronger)
208	  tag-binding ACL — preferred once they leave **Preview**.
209	- **SA `description` sentinel** (`rbk:…`): stable fallback for the sweep; the ledger owns
210	  capability, the description earns exactly this one hint.
211	
212	Federation has no key to leak and a stray principal binding is visible in the policy, so this
213	hazard is citizen-tier-specific.
214	
215	## Authority — who may grant which capability-set
216	
217	- **Resource-scope-split principle.** An authority boundary is IAM-real only where it coincides
218	  with a resource boundary. Payor/governor holds because billing is a separate resource that
219	  governor's `roles/owner` excludes. Governor/director does not — both live in project IAM and
220	  governor has owner over it — so "governor may grant only director/retriever" is, by itself,
221	  honor-system.
222	- **IAM can scope grants by role** (corrects the first paddock draft). The condition attribute
223	  `iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole` limits which roles a setIamPolicy-capable principal
224	  may grant/revoke:
225	  `api.getAttribute('iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole', []).hasOnly(['roles/x','roles/y'])`.
226	  Limits: works only on **project/folder/org** allow policies, **not** resource-level (AR repo,
227	  bucket, individual SA); ≤10 roles per condition; don't join `hasOnly` with `&&`/`||`; some
228	  services don't recognize the limit (request fails); the governor must NOT hold plain
229	  `roles/owner` (unconditional setIamPolicy) — give it a conditioned setIamPolicy instead.
230	  Google's own caveat — don't let a limited admin grant roles bearing setIamPolicy — is satisfied
231	  at *project* scope: the project-scoped director/retriever grants bear no setIamPolicy. The
232	  repo-scoped `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` *does* carry
233	  `artifactregistry.repositories.setIamPolicy` — that is the resource-scope residual covered by the
234	  audit's **member-first axis** (the identity-first axis alone cannot see a grant to a fresh
235	  principal), and modifiedGrantsByRole does not reach repo scope anyway.
236	- **Composed resolution.**
237	  - Project-scoped grants → IAM-limit on the governor (preventive, server-side).
238	  - Resource-scoped grants (repo/bucket/SA) → not IAM-limitable → detective audit (member-first
239	    axis) covers the residual.
240	  - Topology → pull grant authority to the payor where a standing governor buys nothing.
241	- **Tier-dependent.** Keyfile/solo (payor == admin) → topology: a standing owner-holding citizen
242	  buys nothing and is pure attack surface; drop or demote it. Federation/multi-admin (no
243	  exfiltrable key) → a condition-limited governor is the right preventive tool.
244	- **Audit ↔ topology seam.** Surplus-detection's security value depends on topology: a broad
245	  standing governor makes surplus-detection the only escalation tripwire (detective-only, weak vs
246	  malice); authority at the payor closes escalation structurally and demotes the audit to
247	  honest-mistake hygiene. Do not substitute detective audit for structural closure.
248	- **Ephemeral governor** (decoupling-enabled option): grant the governor-set for one op, then
249	  revoke. Caveat: because capability-revoke is decoupled from key-destruction, this strips the
250	  *binding* but leaves the on-disk key — to shrink the keyfile attack surface you must couple
251	  key-destruction (rekey-to-nothing or SA delete) to the revoke; and during grant→use→revoke plus
252	  revoke lag (~81s, memo-20260604) the on-disk key carries full owner. Relevant only if a keyfile
253	  delegated governor is ever wanted.
254	
255	## Blast radius — full reasoning
256	
257	One citizen per person = one SA = one key carrying the **union** of granted capabilities. This is
258	**forced** by "one identity per person": an SA's key always wields the SA's full bindings; you
259	cannot scope one key to a subset. The only escape — separate SAs per role — is the old model,
260	which discards the citizen goal. So accepting multi-role on one citizen *is* accepting the union
261	key; they are one decision.
262	
263	- Cost: larger resting target (a leaked key carries more).
264	- Bounded: the *resting-key* form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only (a file to steal);
265	  federation has no key at rest. The union-of-capabilities-on-one-identity property is itself
266	  tier-blind — a federate granted director+retriever wields both too, exposed through a hijacked
267	  live session rather than a file at rest. Most citizens wear one hat (one-per-person ==
268	  one-per-role).
269	- Upside: finer incident response — revoke a capability without touching the citizen/key, or rekey
270	  without touching capabilities. The old model's only lever was delete-and-recreate.
271	
272	## Governor teardown leak (folds in)
273	
274	`rbgp_governor_mantle` deletes outgoing `governor-*` SAs without first revoking their grants,
275	minting a `deleted:…?uid=` tombstone per re-mantle. The leak closes cheaply: add the generic
276	revoke-before-delete (reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C propagation tolerance) before the
277	existing delete — no tombstone, no naming change. The further idempotent-governor refinement (rekey
278	instead of delete+recreate, dropping the delete entirely) is *not* free: it needs a *stable*
279	governor name in place of today's datestamped `governor-YYYYMMDDHHmm` (which exists precisely to
280	dodge the delete→recreate flap), plus a one-time migration of existing datestamped governors.
281	Standalone leak fix (if the heat descopes the governor):
282	memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.
283	
284	## Citizen naming migration
285	
286	The model's foundation is the SA name becoming **identity-only** (`alice`, not `director-alice`) —
287	the precondition for one-citizen-one-key-union and for the lossy-IAM-derivation argument. (The
288	governor's analogous rename is in Governor teardown leak; this is the director/retriever dual.)
289	Surfaces:
290	
291	- **RBSDK / RBSRK** — SA-name composition drops the role prefix (was `${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>`).
292	- **RBSDD** — email synthesis becomes identity-only.
293	- **RBSDR** — the prefix-filter roster is retired in favor of the declared-ledger read.
294	- **RBSRA / RBSRR** — the three role-keyed `rbra.env` paths collapse toward one identity-keyed path.
295	
296	**Existing-SA cutover.** GCP cannot rename an SA, so legacy `director-*`/`retriever-*` SAs migrate by
297	delete-old + invest-new, re-entering the divest revoke-before-delete + the invest create/propagation
298	race (the memo-20260604 churn) — it must run through the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C tolerance.
299	
300	**One-time bootstrap** (distinct from the *retired* standing "derive from IAM"): recover each legacy
301	citizen's role — and thus its capability-set — from the role-prefixed SA **name** (the RBSDR roster
302	regex), reading bindings only to seed/confirm; thereafter the ledger stands independently. Stamp the
303	orphan marker onto each grandfathered SA so the sweep operates on a marker-uniform domain (a
304	back-stamp prerequisite — until then sweep coverage of the legacy population is incomplete).
305	
306	Whether *this heat* performs the migration is under the open scoping flag (the paddock).
307	
308	## Verified facts (checked 260605)
309	
310	- **Service accounts do not support resource labels.** They support **tags** (tag bindings),
311	  currently in Preview. So the orphan marker uses tags (Preview) or the description sentinel, not a
312	  label.
313	- **IAM can limit which roles a principal may grant** via `modifiedGrantsByRole`, but only on
314	  project/folder/org policies and ≤10 roles — not on resource-level policies.
315	- **A service account is capped at 10 keys** (hard, non-adjustable) — bounds the rekey lifecycle.
316	- A service account **can exist with zero IAM bindings** (the decoupling primitive).
317	
318	Sources:
319	- Set limits on granting roles — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles
320	- Tags for service accounts — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags
321	- Service accounts overview — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-overview
322	
323	## memo-20260527 divergence (record when the model is frozen)
324	
325	memo-20260527 states the operator verbs "keep their names, only their bodies differ," and that
326	keyfile holds "one SA key file each per role." This heat supersedes both: the verb *bodies*
327	converge in shape (not just names), and the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.
328	
329	A third divergence the paddock leans on by name: 20260527's "single code seam" is a *role-keyed*
330	token accessor ("a token as director"), shippable with no behavior change over today's per-role
331	SAs. Under one-SA-per-person the accessor becomes *identity-keyed* ("a token as alice", carrying the
332	union) — a generalization of that seam, but *not* itself no-behavior-change, since it presupposes
333	the per-person migration. (Whether the heat's "load-bearing core" is the role-keyed no-behavior
334	stepping-stone or the identity-keyed end state is an open scoping question — see the paddock.)
335	
336	A deferral-honoring breadcrumb has been added to memo-20260527's Status block now; the full
337	divergence note folds in when the model is frozen.

1	# Operator Credential Models — Keyfile and Workforce Federation
2	
3	Date: 2026-05-27
4	
5	Status: **Committed plan — support two operator-credential models as permanent,
6	per-depot-selected tiers.** The keyfile tier is the shipping default and needs no
7	organization. The federation tier is future build; this memo fixes its shape and
8	the architecture that lets both live in the codebase while never coexisting in a
9	single depot. It is the source document for the RBSHR "Operator federation"
10	roadmap entry.
11	
12	NOTE (2026-06-05): the keyfile-tier shape is under active redesign in heat ₣BZ
13	(`Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md` — the "citizen" model). The
14	one-SA-per-role and verb-naming assumptions here may change; that memo will fold its
15	divergences in once the model is frozen. Not yet revised.
16	
17	---
18	
19	## The plan
20	
21	RBK supports two operator-credential models, permanently, as a free/paid split:
22	
23	- **Keyfile** — service-account key files on disk (RBRA). No GCP organization
24	  required. The free, default tier; ships today.
25	- **Federation** — Workforce Identity Federation against an external IdP. No
26	  secret on disk. Requires a GCP organization. The paid tier.
27	
28	A single per-depot **mode enum** selects which model is in force. One depot runs
29	exactly one model — the two never coexist within an installation — but the
30	codebase carries both forever. The boundary between the tiers is the organization
31	requirement, which is also the natural paywall.
32	
33	---
34	
35	## Two models, two tiers
36	
37	| | **Keyfile** (free) | **Federation** (paid) |
38	|---|---|---|
39	| Operator secret on disk | SA key (RBRA) | none |
40	| GCP organization | not required | required |
41	| Identity proof | possession of the key | fresh IdP sign-in |
42	| Revocation | rotate / delete the key | disable at the IdP |
43	| Roles (gov/dir/ret) | one SA key file each | IAM grants on the operator's principal |
44	
45	Both models terminate in the same artifact: a short-lived Google access token.
46	Everything downstream of the token — every depot API call — is identical and
47	model-blind.
48	
49	---
50	
51	## The organization requirement is the tier boundary
52	
53	Federation's trust setting — the **workforce pool** — can only be created at the
54	**organization** level; a plain project cannot hold one. So federation is
55	structurally org-only, and that requirement is the line between free and paid.
56	
57	An organization is obtainable at no cost via **Cloud Identity Free**, which
58	creates the org once the owner **verifies ownership of a DNS domain**. So the paid
59	tier's real prerequisite is "the operator controls a domain," not "the operator
60	pays Google." The keyfile tier exists to serve operators who cannot or will not
61	verify a domain.
62	
63	---
64	
65	## Workforce vs Workload — two federation products
66	
67	GCP has two federation products answering one question: *what kind of identity is
68	authenticating?*
69	
70	| | **Workforce** Identity Federation | **Workload** Identity Federation |
71	|---|---|---|
72	| For | **Humans** (console, gcloud, API) | **Machines** (CI, apps, cross-cloud) |
73	| Pool scope | **Organization-level** | Project-level |
74	| RBK use | **Operator federation** (this memo) | The **envoy** — GCP→AWS egress |
75	
76	RBK uses both, for different actors; they coexist but never chain for a single
77	actor. The org-level pool scope is why operator federation is org-only.
78	
79	---
80	
81	## How federation authenticates
82	
83	The flow is two legs, both inside RBK's existing curl/openssl/jq stack. gcloud
84	touches **setup only** (pool/provider creation, itself REST- and console-doable),
85	**never runtime**.
86	
87	**Setup (once, Payor-side).** The Payor — as org admin — creates an org-level
88	workforce pool plus a provider trusting an external IdP (any OIDC or SAML 2.0
89	issuer). An **attribute mapping** pins `google.subject` to an *immutable* IdP
90	claim (e.g. `sub`/`oid`, ≤127 bytes); a mutable claim invites spoofing. Operators
91	receive depot capabilities as IAM grants on a **federated principal**:
92	`principal://iam.googleapis.com/locations/global/workforcePools/POOL/subject/SUBJECT`.
93	Identity proofing lives entirely in the IdP; RBK only grants capabilities to an
94	already-known principal, and the depot remains the home of the role-to-identity
95	roster.
96	
97	**Leg 1 — IdP auth (outside Google).** Obtain an OIDC **ID token (JWT)** from the
98	IdP. The headless realization is the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant
99	(RFC 8628):
100	
101	1. The tool POSTs the IdP's device-authorization endpoint → `user_code`,
102	   `verification_uri`, `device_code`.
103	2. The tool prints "go to `<uri>` and enter `WXYZ-1234`." The human authorizes in
104	   **any** browser, on any device — proving themselves to *their own* IdP. Google
105	   is not involved.
106	3. The tool polls the IdP's token endpoint until it returns the ID token.
107	
108	**Leg 2 — STS exchange (pure REST against `sts.googleapis.com`):**
109	
110	```
111	POST https://sts.googleapis.com/v1/token        # send NO Authorization header
112	  grant_type         = urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange   # RFC 8693
113	  audience           = //iam.googleapis.com/locations/global/workforcePools/POOL/providers/PROVIDER
114	  subject_token      = <IdP ID token from Leg 1>
115	  subject_token_type = urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:id_token
116	  → returns a short-lived Google access_token
117	```
118	
119	Then bearer that token (`Authorization: Bearer …`) against any GCP API. Leg 2 has
120	no signing — it just POSTs a token RBK already holds.
121	
122	**Session cap.** A Workforce session is configurable **15 min – 12 h, default
123	1 h** — a hard GCP ceiling. Long-running operation comes from re-running Leg 2,
124	and, where unattended, from a refresh token (test rig only) — never from a single
125	long session.
126	
127	---
128	
129	## IdP landscape
130	
131	Federation trusts any **OIDC or SAML 2.0** issuer — Entra ID, Okta, AD FS, Ping,
132	Auth0, self-hosted Keycloak/Dex, or GCP Identity Platform as an in-house IdP. In
133	practice each operator org uses its existing IdP; RBK only points the pool at it.
134	The headless device flow (RFC 8628) is supported across Entra, Okta, Auth0,
135	Keycloak, and Google, so Leg 1 is curl-friendly on essentially every realistic
136	IdP. The only install where RBK itself chooses the IdP is the project's test org.
137	
138	---
139	
140	## Refresh-token policy
141	
142	A persisted refresh token is the one durable secret federation can reintroduce.
143	Confine it to exactly one place:
144	
145	- **Shipped human operators: no persisted refresh token.** Device flow per
146	  session; reauthentication on the operator org's own cadence.
147	- **Synthetic-human test rig (the project's own test org only): one persisted
148	  refresh token per firewalled machine** — the single acknowledged durable-secret
149	  residual, re-exchanged headless within the session cap. Issuing it requires the
150	  **`offline_access` scope**; without that scope most IdPs return no refresh
151	  token.
152	- **Payor: keeps its own OAuth refresh token** — the bootstrap human identity,
153	  outside the operator mode enum.
154	
155	A persisted refresh token anywhere in the shipped path would recreate the
156	on-disk-secret problem federation exists to remove. Keep it caged in the test rig.
157	
158	---
159	
160	## Where configuration lives
161	
162	Three homes, by scope:
163	
164	- **Depot regime (RBRD)** holds the **mode enum** and, in federation mode, the
165	  org-wide federation facts: **pool, provider, audience**. These are identical for
166	  every operator of the depot. RBRD is tripwire-protected (`rbw-rdc` checks local
167	  `rbrd.env` against the GAR-inscribed copy), so the mode is **tamper-evident**: a
168	  depot cannot be silently downgraded from federation to keyfile without a
169	  deliberate re-inscription. Placing the enum here also makes one-mode-per-depot a
170	  structural fact rather than a per-machine convention — operators cannot
171	  independently straddle modes.
172	- **Station scope (or local)** holds only the per-operator **credential source** —
173	  how *this* machine obtains its IdP token (an interactive session cache, or, for
174	  the test rig, an executable backed by the caged refresh token).
175	- **RBRA files exist only in keyfile mode** — one per role
176	  (governor/director/retriever). In federation mode there are none.
177	
178	There is no separate per-operator credential file: org-wide facts live in RBRD,
179	the one machine-local pointer lives at station scope, and nothing in between needs
180	to persist. A leaked station credential-source pointer is inert — it holds no
181	secret.
182	
183	---
184	
185	## Operator-admin verbs
186	
187	The verbs — **mantle** (Payor→Governor), **invest** (Governor→Director/Retriever),
188	**divest**, **roster** — keep their names, actors, and directions in both models;
189	operator vocabulary does not change with the tier. Only their bodies differ:
190	keyfile mints an SA and downloads a key; federation adds or removes an IAM binding
191	on the operator's `principal://` subject.
192	
193	Federation adds exactly one verb keyfile has no equivalent of: a one-time
194	**Payor-side federation-setup** verb that configures the workforce pool and IdP
195	trust. The test-rig "log a machine in for a month" is a separate **test-only
196	enrollment** verb, never in the shipped register.
197	
198	---
199	
200	## One consumer path, real and synthetic
201	
202	Real and synthetic operators walk the **same consumer path** — read config,
203	resolve a token, call GCP, never knowing what is behind the token. They differ
204	only in the credential source: a real human's resolves against an interactive
205	device-flow session; the synthetic human's resolves against the caged refresh
206	token. This sameness is required, not convenient: CI must exercise the same code
207	path a real operator does, or it validates the wrong thing.
208	
209	---
210	
211	## The single code seam
212	
213	Both models meet at one function: a **role-keyed token accessor** ("give me a
214	token as director"), with the mode enum branching inside it —
215	
216	- **keyfile** → resolve role → RBRA file → signed-JWT exchange;
217	- **federation** → device flow → STS exchange.
218	
219	Downstream is already model-blind: every GCP call takes a bare token string and
220	adds `Authorization: Bearer`, with no knowledge of its origin.
221	
222	Today the keyfile token function is keyed by **file path**, and ~30 call sites
223	pass an explicit RBRA file — so the keyfile concept leaks into every consumer. The
224	enabling step, shippable now with **no behavior change**, is to collapse those
225	call sites behind the role-keyed accessor. After that refactor, federation is a
226	second branch inside one function; the call sites and the HTTP layer never change
227	again.
228	
229	**Sequencing: refactor to the role accessor first (keyfile-only, today), then add
230	the federation branch.**
231	
232	---
233	
234	## Open at implementation time
235	
236	- RBRD field names and any new mints for the mode enum and federation facts.
237	- Concrete credential-source wiring for the interactive human (device-flow output
238	  → what the token resolver consumes).
239	- The chosen IdP(s) for the test org and the federation-setup verb's exact API
240	  calls.
241	
242	---
243	
244	## Sources
245	
246	- [Workforce Identity Federation — overview](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-identity-federation)
247	- [Configure Workforce Identity Federation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/configuring-workforce-identity-federation) — pools, providers, attribute mapping, session duration (15 min – 12 h, default 1 h)
248	- [Obtain short-lived tokens for Workforce Identity Federation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-obtaining-short-lived-credentials) — STS token-exchange, file- and executable-sourced credentials
249	- [Using Workforce Identity Federation with API-based web applications](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/using-workforce-identity-federation-with-api-based-web-applications/) — gcloud-free runtime; IdP-auth then raw STS POST
250	- [Cloud Identity editions](https://docs.cloud.google.com/identity/docs/editions) and [set up Cloud Identity as admin](https://docs.cloud.google.com/identity/docs/how-to/set-up-cloud-identity-admin) — free org via domain verification
251	- [RFC 8628 — OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8628)
252

1	[[rbshr_horizon_roadmap]]
2	==== Horizon Roadmap
3	
4	Defined-but-unscoped future work across Recipe Bottle.
5	Each item has a rationale (why it matters), a trigger (when to revisit), and a reference (where the evidence lives).
6	
7	Items here are not planned for any heat.
8	When an item becomes actionable, it moves into a heat as paces with proper dockets.
9	This is the single collection point for big changes — individual spec subdocuments should cross-reference here rather than maintaining local "Future Work" sections.
10	
11	**SA numeric ID for member references.**
12	Google recommends using a
13	{giam_service_account}'s
14	numeric ID rather than its email address when referencing it immediately after creation, to reduce propagation delay.
15	Recipe Bottle uses email addresses throughout, which is why propagation retry
16	(<<rbscip_trade_study>>
17	backoff profile) is necessary.
18	Switching to numeric ID could reduce retry frequency but would require threading the numeric ID from SA creation responses through all call chains that construct `serviceAccount:` member strings.
19	Requires verification that `setIamPolicy` accepts numeric ID in member strings.
20	_Trigger_: if propagation retries become a significant wall-clock cost in depot creation or governor reset.
21	_Reference_:
22	<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
23	(SA propagation section, evidence table).
24	
25	**Read-back verification universalization.**
26	Currently only
27	{rbtoe_iam_grant_project}
28	performs post-write verification (re-reading policy to confirm the granted role is observable).
29	{rbtoe_iam_grant_repo},
30	{rbtoe_iam_grant_sa},
31	and
32	{rbtoe_iam_grant_bucket}
33	trust the 200 from `setIamPolicy`.
34	Adding verification universally would provide stronger guarantees but adds an HTTP round-trip and a new timeout window per grant.
35	_Trigger_: after etag convergence (\u20a2AsAAF) has been validated in production and we have confidence in the corrected grant contracts.
36	_Reference_:
37	<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
38	(Convergence Analysis section).
39	
40	**SA preflight universalization.**
41	Currently only
42	{rbtoe_iam_grant_sa}
43	performs a pre-flight existence check on the target
44	{giam_service_account}.
45	Applying it to all grant types when the member is a service account requires conditional logic and changes behavior for non-SA members.
46	_Trigger_: if grant failures due to non-existent members become a diagnostic problem.
47	_Reference_:
48	<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
49	(Convergence Analysis section).
50	
51	**Podman as first-class deployment target.**
52	Recipe Bottle currently deploys to Podman on workstations via the bottle lifecycle (RBSBS, RBSBR, RBSBL).
53	Podman support is functional but not hardened — the sentry container, network isolation, and eBPF enforcement assume Linux Podman with rootful access.
54	Expanding to rootless Podman, Podman on macOS (via podman-machine), or alternative container runtimes would require revisiting the security model and network architecture.
55	_Trigger_: user demand for rootless deployment or macOS-native workflows beyond the current Podman VM approach.
56	_Reference_: RBSPV-PodmanVmSupplyChain.adoc (current VM supply chain);
57	RBSBS, RBSBR, RBSBL (bottle lifecycle specs).
58	
59	**`podman-compose` lacks `--wait` for `compose up`.**
60	Docker Compose `--wait` (v2.1.1+) blocks until all container health checks pass, which `rbob_charge` relies on to guarantee crucible readiness before returning.
61	`podman-compose` does not implement `--wait` — the command returns immediately after scheduling containers, creating a race between charge completion and the active-check assertion in theurge.
62	Without `--wait`, Podman integration will need an alternative readiness gate (polling loop, explicit health-check query, or `podman wait --condition=healthy`).
63	_Trigger_: Podman first-class deployment (above).
64	_Reference_: containers/podman-compose#710 (June 2023, open);
65	containers/podman-compose#1329 (October 2025, open).
66	
67	**Revised enshrine — universal capture verb under Lode recipe noun.**
68	Today's narrow `enshrine` mirrors only upstream OCI base images.
69	Three additional capture needs share the same gesture (pull from upstream, SLSA-wrap, store in GAR): build-time tool images (today's `mirror`/`reliquary` machinery), the WSL substrate distro that hosts the Windows workload, and podman VM disk images.
70	The revision generalizes `enshrine` into a universal verb operating on a single recipe noun — **Lode**, parallel to {rbtga_vessel} on the built side — with a kind enum that gates pipeline behavior: `base`, `tool`, `reliquary`, `wsl`, `podvm-wsl`, `podvm-native`.
71	Today's `reliquary` demotes from top-level noun to a kind enum slot — the date-cohort semantic survives, the cult-vocabulary weight drops.
72	Today's `mirror` verb retires entirely;
73	absorbed into enshrine on the `tool` and `reliquary` kinds.
74	Lode .env carries author intent only — no resolved FQIN field — mirroring RBRV's ORIGIN/ANCHOR pattern (kindle code computes upstream coordinates from kind primitives at module-startup).
75	The `wsl` kind delivers a project-controlled WSL rootfs tar that replaces the Microsoft-Store-appx-mutable distribution today's caparison-w depends on;
76	the `podvm-*` kinds collapse `rbv_PodmanVM.sh`'s existing GHCR mirror into GAR under the unified verb.
77	_Trigger_: when Windows host workload requires a project-controlled WSL distro, when podman VM supply chain needs project-owned bytes in GAR, or when the `mirror`/`reliquary` machinery accumulates enough friction to motivate consolidation.
78	_Reference_: RBSAE-ark_enshrine.adoc (current narrow-scope spec);
79	RBSRV-RegimeVessel.adoc (ORIGIN/ANCHOR precedent, kind-enum-gated variable groups pattern);
80	`Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/FUTURE/rbv_PodmanVM.sh` (existing podman-vm fan-out machinery).
81	
82	**Hairpin peer access between crucibles.**
83	Bottled containers cannot currently reach each other's published entry ports.
84	Each bottle is isolated behind its own sentry on a separate enclave network.
85	The traffic path is feasible: Bottle A egresses through Sentry A's transit bridge, the host kernel routes to Sentry B's transit bridge, and Sentry B's existing entry path (DNAT + RBM-FORWARD) accepts the connection unchanged.
86	The security model is preserved — the sentry remains the sole enforcement point, with peer access as a new category of allowed destination alongside entry (inbound) and uplink (outbound internet).
87	Nameplate model: two new variables (`RBRN_PEER_ACCESS_MODE` disabled|allowlist, gated `RBRN_PEER_ALLOWED_MONIKERS` as comma-separated moniker list).
88	No global mode — peer access must always be explicit.
89	Implementation touches three areas: nameplate kindle/validate/render, a peer-access iptables step in security_config, and host-side peer IP discovery via container inspection.
90	Audit gains a cross-instance check: each named peer must exist and have entry enabled.
91	_Trigger_: when a use case requires bottle-to-bottle communication (e.g., microservice composition, service mesh patterns).
92	_Reference_: prior design conversation capture (paddock).
93	
94	**Hoard: external artifact mirror in retention-locked GCS.**
95	A
96	{rbtgi_depot}
97	holding alongside the
98	{rbtga_ark}
99	that mirrors external content-addressed corpora — canonical case: Hugging Face model checkpoints.
100	GCS archive is ~80× cheaper per byte than GAR and supports bucket-level retention locks for compliance-grade immutability that GAR does not offer.
101	Ingress reuses upstream SHA256 addressing (shallow git + LFS pointer resolution, SHA256-keyed GCS objects with natural dedup across revisions);
102	Cloud Build streams so egress stays Google-side;
103	auth path reuses the existing JWT/OAuth bearer curl stack — no new tooling dependency.
104	+
105	Intended consumption: just-in-time workstation mount from the retention-locked bucket into the
106	{at_bottle_container}
107	at charge time, rather than baking models into
108	{at_bottle_image_s}.
109	Keeps images lean, enables model swap without rebuild, keeps the retention property where it naturally lives.
110	Tradeoff: provenance becomes a
111	{at_nameplate}-declared
112	hoard digest recorded at charge time rather than a SLSA-attested image layer — weaker by one link.
113	_Trigger_: first concrete need to run LM inference inside a
114	{at_bottle_container}
115	against a specific HF checkpoint with retention guarantees.
116	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
117	design conversation pending heat.
118	
119	**CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains.**
120	The sentry's iptables CIDR rules accept all protocols and all destination ports to allowed IP ranges.
121	This is intentional: the spec defines
122	{rbrn_uplink_access_mode_allowlist}
123	as "IP access restricted to allowed CIDR ranges" — not port-restricted.
124	The DNS allowlist
125	({rbrn_uplink_allowed_domains})
126	provides name-level precision;
127	the CIDR allowlist provides IP-level gating.
128	These two layers are independent and reinforce each other.
129	+
130	The weakness emerges when allowed domains are CDN-hosted.
131	IANA's example.com (RFC 2606) migrated to Cloudflare, requiring CIDR expansion from `93.184.216.0/24` to include `104.16.0.0/12` — a /12 covering ~1M IPs shared with a large fraction of Cloudflare-fronted sites.
132	The DNS allowlist remains precise (two domains), but the CIDR backstop is now porous: any destination within the Cloudflare range is reachable regardless of domain.
133	A compromised or attacker-controlled site sharing the CDN range would be reachable from the bottle.
134	+
135	Three potential mitigations, in order of increasing ambition:
136	+
137	. **Accept as-is with documentation.**
138	  The DNS allowlist is the primary gate;
139	  the CIDR is a coarse backstop.
140	  Document the known weakness for broad CIDRs.
141	  Suitable when the allowed domains are trusted and the CDN range is acceptable collateral exposure.
142	. **Dynamic CIDR from DNS resolution.**
143	  Have dnsmasq feed resolved IPs into iptables at resolution time, so the allowed set tracks actual DNS responses rather than static configuration.
144	  Eliminates CDN range sprawl.
145	  Requires new sentry infrastructure (dnsmasq hook scripts or ipset integration).
146	. **TLS SNI enforcement.**
147	  Inspect TLS ClientHello for the SNI field and enforce that it matches the DNS allowlist.
148	  Closes the CDN gap at the application layer without dynamic iptables.
149	  Requires a transparent TLS proxy or eBPF-based SNI extraction in the sentry — a different order of complexity.
150	+
151	_Trigger_: when allowed domains require CIDRs broader than /24, or when the threat model requires preventing lateral reachability within a CDN range.
152	_Reference_: rbjs_sentry.sh (Phase 3 CIDR rules);
153	the tadmor nameplate's {rbrn_regime} assignment file (current CIDR configuration).
154	
155	**Encrypted tunnel conduit (WireGuard in sentry).**
156	Add WireGuard to the sentry image so the sentry terminates an encrypted tunnel directly to a VPC hosting PrivateLink endpoints for cloud services.
157	The bottle's traffic routes through a `wg0` interface on the sentry — one stable VPC CIDR replaces floating cloud-provider IP ranges.
158	WireGuard is ~400KB on Alpine, uses static keypair exchange (no PKI/CA), and fits naturally into the sentry's existing privileged-container, explicit-routing architecture.
159	New nameplate fields: tunnel mode, peer endpoint, public keys, allowed IPs.
160	VPC side requires a small WireGuard peer instance and PrivateLink endpoints for target services.
161	_Trigger_: when a bottle needs to reach a cloud service that supports PrivateLink (AWS Bedrock is the known first case; survey should include Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI, GitLab Dedicated, SageMaker, and others as they emerge).
162	_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (full design exploration, approach comparison, service compatibility survey).
163	
164	**Cloud service conduit nameplate.**
165	A nameplate configuration pattern for bottles that access cloud services at runtime.
166	Near-term: allowlist-only nameplate targeting specific service CIDRs and domains (works today with existing sentry machinery, no development needed).
167	Longer-term: tunnel-mode nameplate using WireGuard conduit (above).
168	AWS Bedrock is the known requirement;
169	the pattern should be researched broadly across cloud AI inference services, managed Git hosting, and other PrivateLink-capable endpoints.
170	_Trigger_: immediate — Bedrock access is a current need.
171	Allowlist-only nameplate can be created now;
172	tunnel-mode nameplate follows WireGuard sentry work.
173	_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (approach A for allowlist-only, approach C for tunnel mode).
174	
175	**Bottle credential custody.**
176	Move the service secrets a bottle workload uses — cloud API keys, IAM keys, SSH keys — off the operator's workstation and into the bottle itself, injected at charge time via nameplate/regime configuration rather than baked into the bottle image (a published image must never carry a live secret).
177	The workstation starts the crucible but never holds the service secret; its only credential becomes permission to charge.
178	This blocks credential theft from a compromised workstation — the one threat the sentry allowlist and the encrypted tunnel do not already close — by shrinking the secret's resting place to the contained bottle.
179	The existing RBRA file regime-path pattern provides the model.
180	Orthogonal to but naturally paired with the encrypted tunnel conduit: the tunnel grants the bottle network reach to a cloud service and the sentry custodies the tunnel key, while this item custodies the service secret inside the bottle — network access and the credential to use it configured together.
181	_Trigger_: alongside the conduit nameplate work.
182	_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (Credential Confinement section; the `Credential theft from workstation` row of the threat-model table).
183	
184	NOTE: For SaaS services without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com), the sentry CIDR/domain allowlist + TLS remains the realistic ceiling.
185	The sentry still provides the core value: the bottle cannot reach anything the sentry doesn't allow, and the workstation is cut out of the data path.
186	The tunnel adds defense-in-depth only for PrivateLink-capable services.
187	Cross-reference: the existing "CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains" item (Network Security Model) remains relevant for SaaS-only services where the tunnel cannot help.
188	
189	**Cross-cloud architectural stance: asymmetric sovereignty.**
190	Recipe Bottle governs identities and infrastructure only in GCP.
191	AWS-side presence is shaped as delivered artifacts and policy templates, not owned principals — no AWS-Director, no AWS-Mason, no AWS-Retriever.
192	If Recipe Bottle ever needs to *build* in AWS rather than deliver to it, that would be a deliberate architectural decision triggering a full parallel role hierarchy, not an incremental drift.
193	Vocabulary used in related entries — embassy, envoy, bullion, entrust, accredit, recall, chantry — is tentative working language from a design conversation; concepts are firmer than the words.

199	Provisioned by the payor via operations congruent with
200	{rbtgi_depot}
201	creation — the AWS consumer's embassy is the sovereign-setup equivalent of the Manor's depot.
202	Recipe Bottle ships provisioning templates;
203	the AWS consumer owns the resulting resources.

205	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;

208	**Envoy — GCP-side cloud-native cross-post actor.**
209	A
210	{giam_service_account}
211	in the depot, sibling to existing cloud-native SAs, whose sole purpose is executing cross-cloud pushes to an accredited embassy.
212	The envoy holds no long-lived AWS credentials;
213	it federates to AWS via OIDC Workload Identity Federation, exchanging short-lived GCP tokens for short-lived AWS STS credentials at operation time.
214	Preserves credential locality: Director holds only GCP credentials on the workstation, envoy holds only federation configuration in GCP, and AWS admin credentials never enter Recipe Bottle's custody.
215	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
216	_Reference_: this roadmap entry.
217	
218	**Bullion — AWS-side content-addressed artifact.**

241	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.

244	**Chantry — Bedrock-hosted inference endpoint.**

247	{at_bottle_container}
248	over the crucible conduit architecture — not an artifact Recipe Bottle delivers, but a foreign-sovereign service a bottle consumes.

312	{rbtr_payor}
